arguments.task: Research Sherlock from official and first-party sources. Visit the GitHub repository at https://github.com/sherlock-project/sherlock and any linked official documentation/pages. Determine: one-line description, whether there is a public API and its surface type, authentication method if any, access/pricing/gating, any rate-limit notes, whether an MCP server/integration exists on official GitHub or MCP registry pages, and signals about buildability for an AI-agent toolkit. Prefer repository README, docs, releases, issues/wiki, package pages, and any first-party linked site. Return concise findings with source URLs only from pages actually visited.

input.thought: Start a browser research run on first-party Sherlock sources.

raw_result.description: Open-source OSINT tool that finds usernames across many social networks using public account checks.
raw_result.auth_notes: No end-user account auth is documented for basic use; Sherlock is installed/run locally as a Python CLI or Docker image and queries public sites directly.
raw_result.gating_reason: Project is open source on GitHub with public docs and installation/usage instructions.

api_surface.breadth: No documented hosted API surface was found in the official repository or docs; the documented interface is a local CLI with command-line flags and install options.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No official Sherlock MCP server was evidenced in the repository/docs gathered. A GitHub discussion referenced MCP for a related tool, not Sherlock itself, so MCP existence should be treated as unverified beyond no official evidence found.

raw_result.blocker: No documented hosted API; building an AI-agent toolkit would require wrapping the local CLI/Python package rather than integrating with a self-serve web API.
raw_result.rate_limit_notes: No centralized API rate-limit documentation was found. Because Sherlock checks third-party sites directly, practical limits likely depend on the target websites being queried rather than a Sherlock-managed API.
raw_result.notable_signals: Official docs include install and usage pages plus llms.txt; GitHub repo is public and has releases; the product appears mature as a Python/Docker CLI for OSINT username discovery, but not as a hosted developer platform.
raw_result.human_review_reason: Auth classification and MCP status are somewhat ambiguous because Sherlock is a local open-source CLI rather than a conventional SaaS/API. Evidence supports no documented hosted API and no official MCP, but third-party/community wrappers may exist outside the gathered official evidence.
